5. Versioning: __version__ vs pyproject version consistency.
6. py.typed present so types ship.
7. A real user's first 10 minutes: clone → install → run the reference/example → does it work? Identify every friction point.

Use /Users/thomasjones/hc-assay/.venv/bin/python. Actually TRY a clean-ish install/import and the README steps. Do NOT edit files, do NOT commit.

Return a prioritized list. For EACH gap: title, severity CRITICAL/HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW, file:line/evidence, why it blocks (or harms) public release, concrete fix. Most blocking first.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	# hc-assay
2	
3	**A reusable blueprint for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data.**
4	
5	`hc-assay` is not a single study. It is an **engine + a methodology** that you clone onto a
6	dataset to build an independent, empirical understanding of it — and, where the dataset
7	carries external expert-asserted claims, to adjudicate those claims against that
8	independent understanding.
9	
10	Status: **engine implemented.** The dataset-agnostic engine (`src/assay_engine`) is in place
11	and hardened — the contracts, the methodology core (hypotheses, three verdicts, the two
12	firewalls, the measurement↔interpretation fence), and all five infrastructure seams: the
13	tiered reasoning seam, self-hosted observability (tracing + experiment tracking), persistence
14	(durable checkpointer, vector store, content-addressed versioning), the orchestration graph
15	(gate interrupt/resume), and the baseline toolkit (reproducibility harness + numeric
16	primitives). The engine core is dependency-free; heavy backends are lazy-imported behind
17	optional extras (`reasoning`, `observability`, `persistence`, `orchestration`) so it installs,
18	imports, and unit-tests offline (ADR-0006). Adapters and concrete studies build on top — the
19	next step is the first study instance.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## What it does
24	
25	Given a security & privacy dataset, `hc-assay`:
26	
27	1. **Builds an independent empirical baseline** of the data using ML/NLP — deterministic,
28	   versioned, and reproducible by a hostile reviewer. This baseline is the one object in
29	   the system derived purely from the data.
30	2. Lets the data **surface features, hypotheses, questions, and findings** — discovery
31	   driven by method, not by a pre-conceived narrative.
32	3. **Optionally adjudicates external expert claims.** If the dataset ships claims asserted
33	   by some external authority (relationships, labels, a taxonomy, mappings), those are
34	   converted into typed, falsifiable hypotheses and tested **against the baseline** —
35	   which is kept blind to those claims. Each test returns a verdict: **supported,
36	   contradicted, or indeterminate.**
37	4. **Scores the external source** against the validated baseline — where it aligns, where
38	   it doesn't — as an output of method, with interpretation fenced off from measurement.
39	
40	Everything runs **on-box** (local-first, data-sovereign); nothing leaves the machine.
41	
42	## Two modes
43	
44	- **Discovery** — no external claims; the data surfaces the questions and the findings.
45	- **Adjudicate external claims** — external expert assertions exist and are tested against
46	  the independent baseline. *(Optional, per dataset.)*
47	
48	The external claims are a **pluggable adapter input**. A clone may run pure discovery with
49	no claims at all. The blueprint itself never assumes any particular dataset, claim source,
50	or domain taxonomy.
51	
52	## Engine + adapter
53	
54	- The **engine** is dataset-agnostic and reusable: orchestration, governance gates, the

1	"""assay_engine — the reusable, dataset-agnostic engine of the hc-assay blueprint.
2	
3	The single architectural rule (ADR-0002, ARCHITECTURE.md §3):
4	
5	    The engine never imports dataset specifics. Adapters implement the interfaces in
6	    ``assay_engine.contracts`` that the engine calls. A clone is: implement the adapter,
7	    register it, run.
8	
9	This package contains only goal-agnostic machinery:
10	
11	- ``contracts``      — the engine ↔ adapter boundary (canonical schema + adapter Protocols).
12	- ``methodology``    — hypotheses, the three verdicts, the two firewalls, the
13	                       measurement↔interpretation fence, confirmatory-test machinery.
14	- ``baseline``       — dataset-agnostic baseline builders (embeddings, similarity, graph,
15	                       clustering, descriptive stats).
16	- ``orchestration``  — the analysis graph, phase machine, and governance gates.
17	- ``reasoning``      — the tiered LLM reasoning seam.
18	- ``observability``  — self-hosted tracing + experiment tracking.
19	- ``persistence``    — durable checkpointing, data versioning, vector store.
20	- ``provenance``     — the append-only, hash-chained audit trail (GOVERNANCE §3).
21	- ``pipeline``       — ``run_study``: the composed, governed end-to-end runner (ADR-0010).
22	- ``registry``       — adapter registration.
23	
24	Nothing in this package may import an adapter module or name a specific dataset, authority,
25	or taxonomy.
26	"""
27	
28	__version__ = "0.0.1"
29

1	"""A synthetic, dataset-agnostic reference adapter for exercising the study runner.
2	
3	This is NOT a dataset — it is a minimal, deterministic double that implements every adapter
4	Protocol so the engine's composed pipeline can be run end-to-end (both modes) without any real
5	corpus. It is the runnable demonstration of "the workflow", and the fixture the pipeline tests
6	and the live integration test drive.
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import datetime as _dt
12	import hashlib
13	import json
14	from pathlib import Path
15	from typing import Iterable
16	
17	from assay_engine.baseline.determinism import build_baseline_artifact
18	from assay_engine.baseline.toolkit import BaselineArtifact
19	from assay_engine.contracts.claims import ClaimRecord
20	from assay_engine.contracts.schema import Corpus, Unit
21	from assay_engine.contracts.study import StudyDefinition, StudyMode
22	from assay_engine.methodology.firewalls import ClaimBlindGuard, DiscoverConfirmSplit
23	from assay_engine.methodology.hypothesis import Hypothesis, HypothesisKind, HypothesisOrigin
24	from assay_engine.methodology.preregistration import LocalHmacAuthority, lock_hypothesis
25	from assay_engine.methodology.verdict import Verdict
26	from assay_engine.pipeline import StudyPlan
27	
28	AUTHORITY = LocalHmacAuthority(b"reference-study-secret-key-000001")
29	_PAST = _dt.datetime.now(tz=_dt.timezone.utc) - _dt.timedelta(hours=1)
30	
31	
32	def write_source(path: Path, n_units: int = 6) -> Path:
33	    """Write a tiny JSON corpus source the ReferenceParser can ingest deterministically."""
34	    units = [{"id": f"u{i}", "text": f"control text number {i}"} for i in range(n_units)]
35	    path.write_text(json.dumps({"units": units}), encoding="utf-8")
36	    return path
37	
38	
39	class ReferenceParser:
40	    def parse(self, source: Path) -> Corpus:
41	        data = json.loads(Path(source).read_text(encoding="utf-8"))
42	        return Corpus(units=tuple(Unit(u["id"], u["text"]) for u in data["units"]))
43	
44	    def source_fingerprint(self, source: Path) -> str:
45	        return hashlib.sha256(Path(source).read_bytes()).hexdigest()
46	
47	
48	class ReferenceBaselineBuilder:
49	    """Blind baseline: a deterministic summary stat over the corpus text only."""
50	
51	    def build(
52	        self, corpus: Corpus, *, claim_guard: ClaimBlindGuard[object]
53	    ) -> BaselineArtifact:
54	        mean_len = sum(len(u.text) for u in corpus.units) / max(1, len(corpus.units))
55	        return build_baseline_artifact(
56	            corpus, {"mean_text_len": mean_len, "n_units": len(corpus.units)},
57	            component_versions={"builder": "reference@1"},
58	        )
59	
60	
61	class ReferenceClaims:
62	    def __init__(self, claim_ids: tuple[str, ...] = ("c1", "c2")) -> None:
63	        self._ids = claim_ids
64	
65	    def claims(self) -> Iterable[ClaimRecord]:
66	        return [
67	            ClaimRecord(claim_id=c, subject=c, referents=(c,), assertion={"expected": "high"})
68	            for c in self._ids
69	        ]
70	
71	    def claim_fingerprint(self) -> str:
72	        return hashlib.sha256(",".join(self._ids).encode()).hexdigest()
73	
74	
75	def discover(discovery_corpus: Corpus) -> list[Hypothesis]:
76	    """Data-surface one whole-corpus hypothesis, locked (pre-registered) in the past."""
77	    h = Hypothesis(
78	        hypothesis_id="H-disc-1",
79	        statement="discovery-partition units share above-chance internal similarity",
80	        kind=HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS, origin=HypothesisOrigin.DISCOVERY,
81	        test_name="permutation", decision_rule="empirical p<=0.05 in the 'greater' tail",
82	        predicted_direction="greater",
83	    )
84	    return [lock_hypothesis(h, authority=AUTHORITY, instant=_PAST)]
85	
86	
87	def confirm_held_out(hypothesis: Hypothesis, held_out: Corpus) -> Verdict:
88	    # a study computes its real statistic on held_out; here a deterministic stand-in verdict
89	    assert held_out.units, "confirm must receive a non-empty held-out partition"
90	    return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, "held-out test passed (reference)")
91	
92	
93	def hypothesis_for(claim: ClaimRecord) -> Hypothesis:
94	    """Map a claim to its pre-stated, locked EXTERNAL_CLAIM hypothesis (locked pre-baseline)."""
95	    h = Hypothesis(
96	        hypothesis_id=f"H-{claim.claim_id}",
97	        statement="claim holds against the blind baseline",
98	        kind=HypothesisKind.WHOLE_CORPUS, origin=HypothesisOrigin.EXTERNAL_CLAIM,
99	        test_name="adjudication", decision_rule="baseline corroborates the asserted relationship",
100	        source_claim_id=claim.claim_id, predicted_direction="greater",
101	    )
102	    return lock_hypothesis(h, authority=AUTHORITY, instant=_PAST)
103	
104	
105	def confirm_claim(hypothesis: Hypothesis, baseline: BaselineArtifact, claim: ClaimRecord) -> Verdict:
106	    assert "mean_text_len" in baseline.contents  # the real baseline is passed through
107	    return Verdict.supported(hypothesis.hypothesis_id, "baseline corroborates (reference)")
108	
109	
110	def make_plan(source: Path, *, modes: frozenset[StudyMode]) -> StudyPlan:
111	    """Assemble a StudyPlan over the reference adapter for the requested modes."""
112	    adjudicate = StudyMode.ADJUDICATE_EXTERNAL_CLAIMS in modes
113	    discovery = StudyMode.DISCOVERY in modes
114	    definition = StudyDefinition(
115	        name="reference-study",
116	        modes=modes,
117	        parser=ReferenceParser(),
118	        research_questions=("does the reference pipeline run end-to-end?",),
119	        claims_source=ReferenceClaims() if adjudicate else None,
120	    )
121	    split = (
122	        DiscoverConfirmSplit.from_partition({"u0", "u1", "u2"}, {"u3", "u4", "u5"})
123	        if discovery else None
124	    )
125	    return StudyPlan(
126	        definition=definition, source=source,
127	        baseline_builder=ReferenceBaselineBuilder(), authority=AUTHORITY,
128	        split=split,
129	        discover=discover if discovery else None,
130	        confirm_held_out=confirm_held_out if discovery else None,
131	        hypothesis_for=hypothesis_for if adjudicate else None,
132	        confirm_claim=confirm_claim if adjudicate else None,
133	    )
134

145	def run_study(
146	    plan: StudyPlan,
147	    *,
148	    tracer: Tracer | None = None,
149	    gate_handler: GateHandler = auto_approve,
150	    clock: Clock | None = None,
151	    trail: ProvenanceTrail | None = None,
152	) -> StudyResult:
153	    """Run one study end-to-end through its governed phase sequence. See module docstring.
154	
155	    ``tracer`` defaults to :class:`OtelTracer` (real spans if OpenTelemetry + a provider are
156	    present, a graceful no-op otherwise). ``gate_handler`` decides the human governance gates
157	    (default auto-approves, recording the decision). ``clock`` injects a deterministic clock for
158	    the provenance timestamps only (ordering checks always use real time).
159	
160	    ``trail`` may be a caller-owned :class:`ProvenanceTrail` (optionally keyed): the runner
161	    records into it, so if the run *raises* (a blocked gate, a firewall violation) the caller
162	    still holds the partial trail — including the blocking decision — for audit (#92). If
163	    omitted, an internal unkeyed trail is used and surfaced only on success.
164	
165	    Raises ``FirewallViolation``/``PreRegistrationError`` on any methodological breach,
166	    ``GateError`` if a gate blocks a transition, and ``ValueError`` on a malformed run.
167	    """
168	    tracer = tracer or OtelTracer()
169	    defn = plan.definition
170	    modes = defn.modes
171	    required = required_phases(modes)
172	    trail = trail if trail is not None else ProvenanceTrail(clock=clock)
173	    visited: list[Phase] = []
174	
175	    def enter(phase: Phase, **payload: Any) -> None:
176	        visited.append(phase)
177	        trail.record("phase", f"enter {phase.name}", phase=phase.name, **payload)
178	
179	    def run_gate(review: GateReview) -> None:
180	        """Invoke the governance gate, recording the decision once and blocking on rejection.
181	
182	        The decision's ``approved``/``reason`` are read EXACTLY ONCE (snapshotted) and used for
183	        both the recorded entry and the control-flow branch, so a side-effecting decision object
184	        cannot diverge what is recorded from what is enforced (#94).

# hc-assay

**A reusable blueprint for rigorous, reproducible empirical ML/NLP on security & privacy data.**

`hc-assay` is not a single study. It is an **engine + a methodology** that you clone onto a
dataset to build an independent, empirical understanding of it — and, where the dataset
carries external expert-asserted claims, to adjudicate those claims against that
independent understanding.

Status: **engine implemented.** The dataset-agnostic engine (`src/assay_engine`) is in place
and hardened — the contracts, the methodology core (hypotheses, three verdicts, the two
firewalls, the measurement↔interpretation fence), and all five infrastructure seams: the
tiered reasoning seam, self-hosted observability (tracing + experiment tracking), persistence
(durable checkpointer, vector store, content-addressed versioning), the orchestration graph
(gate interrupt/resume), and the baseline toolkit (reproducibility harness + numeric
primitives). The engine core is dependency-free; heavy backends are lazy-imported behind
optional extras (`reasoning`, `observability`, `persistence`, `orchestration`) so it installs,
imports, and unit-tests offline (ADR-0006). Adapters and concrete studies build on top — the
next step is the first study instance.

---

## What it does

Given a security & privacy dataset, `hc-assay`:

1. **Builds an independent empirical baseline** of the data using ML/NLP — deterministic,
   versioned, and reproducible by a hostile reviewer. This baseline is the one object in
   the system derived purely from the data.
2. Lets the data **surface features, hypotheses, questions, and findings** — discovery
   driven by method, not by a pre-conceived narrative.
3. **Optionally adjudicates external expert claims.** If the dataset ships claims asserted
   by some external authority (relationships, labels, a taxonomy, mappings), those are
   converted into typed, falsifiable hypotheses and tested **against the baseline** —
   which is kept blind to those claims. Each test returns a verdict: **supported,
   contradicted, or indeterminate.**
4. **Scores the external source** against the validated baseline — where it aligns, where
   it doesn't — as an output of method, with interpretation fenced off from measurement.

Everything runs **on-box** (local-first, data-sovereign); nothing leaves the machine.

## Two modes

- **Discovery** — no external claims; the data surfaces the questions and the findings.
- **Adjudicate external claims** — external expert assertions exist and are tested against
  the independent baseline. *(Optional, per dataset.)*

The external claims are a **pluggable adapter input**. A clone may run pure discovery with
no claims at all. The blueprint itself never assumes any particular dataset, claim source,
or domain taxonomy.

## Engine + adapter

- The **engine** is dataset-agnostic and reusable: orchestration, governance gates, the
  reasoning seam, observability, persistence, the baseline toolkit, and the methodology
  core (hypothesis typing, three-verdict testing, the firewalls, the measurement↔
  interpretation fence).
- An **adapter** is what you write when you clone onto a new dataset: an ingestion parser,
  the canonical-schema binding, an optional external-claims source, a domain glossary /
  feature builders, and that study's pre-registration.

Each concrete study lives in **its own repository** built on this engine, to keep every
study's pre-registration clean and independent.

## Documentation

| Doc | Purpose |
|---|---|
| [docs/CHARTER.md](docs/CHARTER.md) | Purpose, principles, scope, operating model |
| [docs/METHODOLOGY.md](docs/METHODOLOGY.md) | The research method, the two firewalls, verdicts, reproducibility |
| [docs/ARCHITECTURE.md](docs/ARCHITECTURE.md) | Engine vs adapter, components, the adapter contract, onboarding |
| [docs/GOVERNANCE.md](docs/GOVERNANCE.md) | Gates, pre-registration, provenance, independence, data sovereignty |
| [docs/GLOSSARY.md](docs/GLOSSARY.md) | Canonical terms |
| [docs/decisions/](docs/decisions/) | Architecture Decision Records |

content.content:
## 2. The adapter (per-dataset — what a clone writes)

| Adapter piece | Responsibility |
|---|---|
| **Ingestion parser** | Raw source → the canonical schema. The only place that understands the source's native format. |
| **Canonical-schema binding** | Maps the dataset's entities/fields onto the engine's canonical types. |
| **External-claims source** *(optional)* | If the dataset ships external expert-asserted claims, this exposes them so the engine can convert each into a typed hypothesis. Absent for pure-discovery datasets. **Quarantined from baseline construction** (Firewall A). |
| **Domain glossary / feature builders** | Dataset-specific vocabulary handling and any features only meaningful for this dataset. |
| **Study definition** | The study's research questions and its pre-registration. |

## 3. The engine ↔ adapter contract

- The engine depends only on **interfaces** (canonical schema types, an optional
  claims-provider interface, a feature-builder interface). It imports no adapter module.
- Adapters depend on the engine, never the reverse, and never on each other.
- The external-claims source is structurally separable so the baseline pipeline can be run
  with it withheld — that is how Firewall A (claim-blindness) is enforced, not just
  promised.

## 4. Onboarding a new dataset

1. Create a new instance repository on the engine.
2. Implement the adapter (parser, schema binding, optional claims source, glossary).
3. Write the study definition (questions + pre-registration).
4. Run: the engine builds the baseline, runs discovery and/or claim-adjudication, gates the
   transitions, records provenance, and emits verdicts and (if applicable) the source
   scorecard.

## 5. Technical substrate

Local-first, on-box, data-sovereign:

- Python; LangGraph / LangChain for the analysis graph and agent orchestration.
- Reasoning seam over a local LLM runtime (bulk) and a frontier model via fixed-cost
  subscription (high-stakes) — no metered API.
- Self-hosted OpenTelemetry tracing collector + a local experiment-tracking store.
- Durable checkpointer (local database), data versioning, and a local vector store.

Specific tools are engine implementation details and may evolve; the engine/adapter
boundary and the methodology core are the stable contract.

# Audit: hc-assay public-release gaps (packaging / distribution / docs / CI / hygiene)

I performed a clean `git clone` to `/tmp/assay_clone`, built a fresh Python 3.12 venv via `uv`, and actually ran: dep-free install, import, `ruff`, `mypy`, `pytest`, a wheel build, and the reference study end-to-end. The engine machinery is genuinely solid — dep-free install works, 319 tests pass, ruff/mypy clean, `py.typed` ships. The gaps are all in **license, docs-for-users, and repo hygiene**, and three of them hard-block making this public.

## CRITICAL

### 1. License is "Proprietary" — legally blocks open-sourcing
- Evidence: `pyproject.toml:11` → `license = { text = "Proprietary" }`; wheel METADATA emits `License: Proprietary`. No `LICENSE` file exists anywhere (`ls LICENSE*` → no matches in clean clone).
- Why it blocks: Publishing a repo with no license = "all rights reserved" by default; declaring it `Proprietary` is worse — it actively tells users they may not use/copy/fork it. A public repo with this metadata is contradictory and unusable by anyone.
- Fix: Decide the actual license (Apache-2.0 or MIT are typical for a reusable engine). Add a `LICENSE` file at repo root, and set `license = "Apache-2.0"` (or via SPDX expression) plus a `License ::` classifier in `pyproject.toml`. Make the file and the metadata agree.

### 2. A binary database (`assay_mlflow.db`, 260 KB) is committed and ships in every clone
- Evidence: `git ls-files` includes `assay_mlflow.db`; clean clone contains it; committed in `ff827ae` (#41), so it's in history too. `.gitignore` ignores `data/`, `*.parquet`, etc. but not `*.db` / `assay_mlflow.db` / `mlruns/`.
- Why it blocks: It's a run-output artifact (MLflow experiment store) with no business in source control — bloats the repo, may contain run metadata/paths, and signals sloppy hygiene on a repo whose entire selling point is rigor and provenance. (Good news: `mlruns/` and `.coverage` are NOT tracked, only this DB is.)
- Fix: `git rm --cached assay_mlflow.db`; add `assay_mlflow.db`, `*.db`, and `mlruns/` to `.gitignore`. Because it's in history, scrub it from history (`git filter-repo --path assay_mlflow.db --invert-paths`) before going public, or accept it lives in history forever.

### 3. README has no install/quickstart/example — and there is no runnable example a user can find
- Evidence: `grep -niE "pip install|quickstart|run_study|example"` over `README.md` → zero hits. The only runnable demonstration is `tests/reference_study.py`, which (a) lives under `tests/` so it isn't shipped in the wheel, and (b) is never referenced from any user-facing doc. To run it I had to `sys.path.insert(0, "tests")` and reverse-engineer `make_plan`/`write_source`/`run_study` from source.
- Why it blocks: A public engine whose pitch is "clone it onto your dataset" with no shown install command, no `run_study` example, and no adapter snippet fails the "first 10 minutes" test completely. `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md §2-4` describes the adapter *conceptually* but contains no code.
- Fix: Add a Quickstart to `README.md`: `pip install -e ".[dev]"`, a ~20-line minimal adapter (parser + baseline builder + `StudyDefinition` + `StudyPlan`), and `run_study(plan)` showing how to read `StudyResult` (`.scorecard`, `.discovery_verdicts`, `.provenance`). Either promote the reference adapter into `docs/`/an `examples/` dir or link it explicitly and explain how to run it.

## HIGH

### 4. No `all` extra despite the audit's expectation and common UX
- Evidence: `uv pip install -e ".[all]"` → `warning: ... does not have an extra named 'all'`. `pyproject.toml:19-50` defines `dev, reasoning, observability, persistence, orchestration, baseline` but no aggregate.
- Why it harms: Users can't get the full backend stack in one command; they must memorize and chain six extras (as CI does at `ci.yml:63`). Standard convenience for a multi-extra package.
- Fix: Add `all = ["assay-engine[reasoning,observability,persistence,orchestration,baseline]"]` (self-referential extra) to `[project.optional-dependencies]`. Then `ci.yml:63` can simplify to `.[dev,all]`.

### 5. pyproject metadata is thin — no classifiers, no project.urls, weak authors
- Evidence: `pyproject.toml:5-17`. Missing `[project.urls]` (Homepage/Repository/Issues), missing `classifiers` (no Python versions, no license, no Development Status, no topic), `authors = [{ name = "hc-assay" }]` has no email; wheel METADATA shows empty `Home-page`.
- Why it harms: For a public/PyPI-bound package these are expected discoverability/trust signals; their absence reads as unfinished.
- Fix: Add `classifiers` (Development Status, supported `Programming Language :: Python :: 3.11/3.12/3.13`, `License ::`, `Intended Audience`, `Topic`), add `[project.urls]` with the repo URL, and a real author/maintainer with email.

## MEDIUM

### 6. `requires-python` allows 3.14 but CI never tests it (and 3.14 is the only local interpreter)
- Evidence: `pyproject.toml:10` → `>=3.11,<3.15` (so 3.14 is "supported"); `ci.yml:15` matrix is only `["3.11","3.12","3.13"]`. The dev `.venv` is 3.14.5.
- Why it harms: You're advertising 3.14 support with zero CI coverage; a 3.14-only breakage would ship undetected. Conversely, if 3.14 isn't intended, the cap is wrong.
- Fix: Either add `"3.14"` to the CI matrix (both lanes), or tighten the cap to `<3.14`. Make the advertised range and the tested range identical.

### 7. No CONTRIBUTING / SECURITY / CODE_OF_CONDUCT
- Evidence: none present (`ls` → no matches).
- Why it harms: Nice-to-have for public repos; SECURITY.md especially matters for a security-domain project (where do people report vulns?).
- Fix: Add minimal `CONTRIBUTING.md` (dev setup: `uv venv`, `pip install -e ".[dev]"`, run ruff/mypy/pytest), `SECURITY.md` (private disclosure contact), and a standard `CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md`.

## LOW

### 8. CI doesn't enforce `ruff format` and pins no tool floor reproducibly
- Evidence: `ci.yml:25-28` runs `ruff check` and `mypy` but never `ruff format --check`; `[tool.ruff]` (`pyproject.toml:59-61`) sets only line-length, no `select` rule set, so linting is the default-minimal rule set.
- Why it harms: Formatting drift won't fail CI; a thin lint config catches less than a public repo usually wants. Not blocking — current code is clean.
- Fix (optional): Add `ruff format --check src tests` to the core lane and consider an explicit `[tool.ruff.lint] select = [...]`.

## Things that are GOOD (verified, no action needed)
- Dep-free `pip install -e ".[dev]"` works; `import assay_engine` works; `__version__ == "0.0.1"` matches `pyproject.toml:7` (consistent).
